behaviors.description: Command segmentation treats newline-separated statements as separate segments, not one monolithic block.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is entirely within the segmentation algorithm — a string-splitting or shell-parsing function that incorrectly lumps newline-separated statements together. No module boundary is crossed. REAL-INFRA: No I/O, OS, or subprocess required; the function takes a string and returns a list. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing to mock. LLM-DEP: No AI/ML involved. Rejection of Integration: there is no handoff between modules to test here — a single parsing function is the complete failure site.
behaviors.test_description: Call the segmentation function with a multi-line input string such as `'VAR=value\ngh pr create --title foo'` and assert that the result is two distinct segments (`['VAR=value', 'gh pr create --title foo']`), not one monolithic string.

behaviors.description: A multi-line shell block with variable assignments followed by `gh pr create` is recognized as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the trigger-detection/classification logic applied to an already-segmented (or raw) command block. This is a pure matching/recognition algorithm operating on a string. REAL-INFRA: No I/O needed; the classifier consumes a string and returns a classification. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the segmentation output with a simple list stub would not hide the detection failure — the stub is the input, and we are testing whether the detector finds the trigger. LLM-DEP: No AI/ML. Rejection of Integration: segmentation and detection may be two functions, but the test input can be provided directly to the detector; no state propagation between modules is under test here.
behaviors.test_description: Pass the full multi-line string `'VAR1=foo\nVAR2=bar\ngh pr create --title "My PR" --body "desc"'` directly to the PR-trigger detection function and assert it returns a PR-create trigger classification (not `not_a_trigger`).

behaviors.description: A similar multi-line block that ends with `gh issue create` is not incorrectly treated as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This is the negative-case boundary of the same detection algorithm tested in behavior 2 — the failure is a false-positive classification inside a single function. REAL-INFRA: Same string-in / classification-out shape; no I/O. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing meaningful to mock. LLM-DEP: No AI/ML. Rejection of Integration: identical reasoning to behavior 2; the module boundary is irrelevant because the input can be injected directly into the classifier.
behaviors.test_description: Pass `'VAR1=foo\nVAR2=bar\ngh issue create --title "My Issue"'` to the PR-trigger detection function and assert it returns `not_a_trigger` (or equivalent non-PR-create classification), confirming the detector does not fire on `gh issue create`.

behaviors.description: When a PR-create trigger is detected, the hook can reconstruct and persist the PR identifier needed by the review loop state.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The behavior spans two concerns: (a) extracting the PR identifier from the command and (b) writing it to a state file. The failure mode the incident exposed is that the whole block was misclassified and no file was written — confirming the bug only manifests when detection and persistence are wired together end-to-end. Testing detection alone (Unit) does not verify that the identifier is correctly extracted and handed to the persistence layer. REAL-INFRA: Persistence requires a real filesystem write (a tmpdir is sufficient; no OS-level events or subprocesses are needed), placing this at Integration. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the file-write would trivially pass without proving the identifier is correctly reconstructed and serialized. LLM-DEP: No AI/ML. Rejection of System: a real tmpdir is sufficient; no subprocess, network, or special OS primitives are required.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the PostToolUse hook's detection + persistence pipeline with a multi-line PR-create command string (variable assignments + `gh pr create ...`) against a real temporary directory. Assert that the expected state file is created in that directory and that its contents include the correct PR identifier extracted from the command.

behaviors.description: After PR-create detection, the review gate state is written in a form that the pre-tool enforcement hook can read and enforce.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is a format mismatch between what the PostToolUse hook writes and what the pre-tool enforcement hook reads — a module-boundary contract. This only surfaces when both modules are exercised against the same state file. REAL-INFRA: Both hooks operating on a shared tmpdir is sufficient; no subprocess invocation or real network is required if the hooks expose importable read/write/enforce functions. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the file read in the enforcement hook would hide any serialization or schema incompatibility between the two hooks. LLM-DEP: No AI/ML. Rejection of System: if both hooks are importable Python modules, an in-process tmpdir roundtrip catches the format contract failure without spawning subprocesses. Rejection of Unit: the bug exists at the boundary between two distinct hook modules — a single module test cannot catch a write-format / read-format mismatch.
behaviors.test_description: Using a shared temporary directory: (1) invoke the PostToolUse hook's state-write path with a detected PR-create trigger to produce the gate state file; (2) invoke the pre-tool enforcement hook's read-and-enforce path pointing at the same directory; assert that the enforcement hook correctly identifies the gate as active and would block tool use, confirming the written format is parseable and actionable by the enforcer.
